items.name: SEO Brief Prompt Pack
items.tagline: Five chained prompts that turn a keyword and a SERP into a review-gated SEO brief.
items.what:
A five-prompt pack that walks a target keyword all the way to a writer-ready SEO brief: keyword-to-intent analysis, an outline with heading hierarchy, an entity and subtopic coverage checklist, an internal-link plan drawn from your own URLs, and title/meta variants. Each prompt names exactly what to paste in and what shape the output takes, so the results drop straight into a standardized brief.

It exists because briefing is a common bottleneck in content operations. The pack makes that step fast and repeatable without lowering the bar — every draft passes an editor before a writer ever sees it, and every run is logged so a brief can be traced and regenerated.
whenToUse: You have a target keyword and need a structured brief a writer can act on without guessing
You are producing briefs by hand in an AI assistant and want a saved, repeatable prompt sequence instead of ad-hoc requests
You want intent classification and SERP analysis captured the same way every time, so quality survives staff turnover
You are proving the manual version of a content-brief automation before wiring up any workflow tooling
items.artifact:
# SEO Brief Prompt Pack

Five chained prompts that take one target keyword to a writer-ready SEO brief:
intent -> outline -> coverage checklist -> internal-link plan -> title/meta variants.
Run them in order. Each output feeds the next. Nothing here publishes anything —
the pack produces a draft brief that an editor signs off before a writer sees it.

---

## How to use this pack

1. Fill every {{PLACEHOLDER}} the first time it appears. Later prompts reuse
   values you already produced (intent, outline, word-count target).
2. Paste one prompt at a time into your assistant. Copy each output into the
   brief template at the bottom of this file.
3. Keep prompts read-only. You paste data in; the assistant analyzes and drafts.
   It is not connected to your CMS and cannot publish — by design. If you run
   this pack through an agent instead of a chat session, the agent operates
   under your Campaign AGENTS.md scope rules: pasted SERPs and URL exports are
   read-only sources, and every brief is RECOMMEND-tier output.
4. Log the run (see "Audit log line") and route the finished draft to review.
   No brief moves to a writer until an editor approves it.

---

## Governance rail (fill once, applies to the whole pack)

- REVIEW GATE: Every brief is a draft until {{REVIEWER_NAME}} (the editor who
  approves briefs) signs off. Drafts never publish and never reach a writer
  unreviewed.
- READ-ONLY DEFAULT: This pack only reads what you paste (keyword, SERP titles,
  your URL list). It gets no login and no publish scope of any kind.
- CAPS (placeholders — set your own house numbers, these are not recommendations):
    - {{MAX_INTERNAL_LINKS}} (max internal links proposed per brief)
    - {{MAX_H2}} (max H2 sections in an outline)
    - {{TITLE_CHAR_CAP}} (max title length you allow)
    - {{META_CHAR_CAP}} (max meta description length you allow)
- AUDIT LINE: Log every brief with the Trigger / Action / Impact line at the end
  of this file, so any brief can be traced back to its inputs and regenerated.

Shared inputs you will paste more than once:
- {{TARGET_KEYWORD}} (the primary keyword this brief targets)
- {{BRAND}} (your brand or site name)
- {{DOMAIN}} (your domain, e.g. example.com)

---

## Prompt 1 — Keyword-to-intent analysis

**Inputs:**
- {{TARGET_KEYWORD}}
- {{SERP_TITLES}} (paste the titles of the top 10 organic results, one per line)
- {{AUDIENCE}} (one line: who you are writing for, e.g. "B2B ops leads")

**Prompt:**

```
You are an SEO strategist. Analyze search intent for a single keyword. Use ONLY
the SERP titles I provide as evidence — do not invent rankings or metrics.

Keyword: {{TARGET_KEYWORD}}
Audience: {{AUDIENCE}}
Top SERP titles (one per line):
{{SERP_TITLES}}

Classify the dominant intent as exactly one of: Informational, Commercial,
Navigational, or Transactional. Then justify it from patterns in the titles.

Return a Markdown table with these rows:
| Field | Value |
| Dominant intent | (one label) |
| Confidence | High / Medium / Low, with one-line reason |
| Evidence from titles | 2-3 recurring patterns you see (formats, modifiers, angle) |
| Content format implied | e.g. how-to guide, listicle, comparison, definition |
| Suggested angle | one sentence — how {{BRAND}} can differ from the SERP |
| Word-count target | a range, inferred from the depth the titles imply, marked "estimate" |
| Open questions for editor | anything the titles cannot settle |

Do not write the article. Do not claim search volume or difficulty numbers.
```

**Expected output:** the 7-row table above. Save "Dominant intent" and "Word-count
target" — Prompts 2 and 3 reuse them.

**Guardrail:** intent is a judgment call. {{REVIEWER_NAME}} confirms the label and
the angle before you build the outline — a wrong intent here misroutes the whole
brief.

---

## Prompt 2 — Outline with heading hierarchy

**Inputs:**
- {{TARGET_KEYWORD}}
- {{SEARCH_INTENT}} (the label from Prompt 1)
- {{ANGLE}} (the suggested angle from Prompt 1, edited if the editor changed it)
- {{WORD_COUNT_TARGET}} (the range from Prompt 1)
- {{SERP_H2_THEMES}} (optional: recurring H2 themes you noted while scanning the SERP)

**Prompt:**

```
You are building the outline for one SEO article. Produce a heading hierarchy
only — no body copy.

Keyword: {{TARGET_KEYWORD}}
Intent: {{SEARCH_INTENT}}
Angle: {{ANGLE}}
Word-count target: {{WORD_COUNT_TARGET}}
Themes seen in competing results: {{SERP_H2_THEMES}}

Rules:
- One H1 that contains the keyword naturally.
- No more than {{MAX_H2}} H2 sections. Nest H3s only where a section truly splits.
- Each heading gets a one-line "covers:" note stating what the section answers.
- Order sections to match the intent (definition-first for informational,
  comparison-first for commercial, etc.).
- Mark one section as the primary answer target for a featured snippet, if any.

Return this structure:

  # H1: (title-style heading)
  ## H2: (heading) — covers: (one line)
     ### H3: (heading) — covers: (one line)
  ## H2: ...

End with a one-line "Notes for writer" on tone or a must-include example.
```

**Expected output:** an indented H1/H2/H3 tree, each heading with a "covers:" note,
plus one "Notes for writer" line.

**Guardrail:** the editor checks that the hierarchy is logical, stays within
{{MAX_H2}} H2s, and that no heading promises content the intent does not support.

---

## Prompt 3 — Entity & subtopic coverage checklist

**Inputs:**
- {{TARGET_KEYWORD}}
- {{SEARCH_INTENT}} (from Prompt 1)
- {{OUTLINE}} (paste the full outline from Prompt 2)

**Prompt:**

```
You are doing topical-coverage QA on an article outline before it is written.
Identify the entities and subtopics a thorough piece on this keyword should
cover, then check them against the outline.

Keyword: {{TARGET_KEYWORD}}
Intent: {{SEARCH_INTENT}}
Outline:
{{OUTLINE}}

List the entities, concepts, and subtopics a strong article should address.
Base this on the keyword's topic, not on external data you cannot verify.
Do not fabricate statistics, dates, or named studies for the writer to cite.

Return a Markdown table:
| Entity / subtopic | Why it matters | Covered in outline? | Where / gap note |
- "Covered in outline?" = Yes / Partial / Missing
- For Partial or Missing, say which section should hold it.

After the table, add:
- "Must-add before writing" — any Missing item that is core to the intent.
- "Nice-to-have" — items that would strengthen but are optional.
```

**Expected output:** the coverage table plus two short lists (must-add, nice-to-have).

**Guardrail:** the editor decides which "must-add" items are real gaps vs. scope
creep, and folds the accepted ones back into the Prompt 2 outline before the brief
is finalized. Any statistic a "must-add" item implies must be source-checked by a
human, not taken from the model.

---

## Prompt 4 — Internal-link plan

**Inputs:**
- {{TARGET_KEYWORD}}
- {{OUTLINE}} (from Prompt 2, with any accepted gaps added)
- {{EXISTING_URLS}} (paste your site's URL inventory, one per line, as
  "URL — page title or topic". Use a sitemap export or crawl. Read-only.)

**Prompt:**

```
You are planning internal links for a new article. Choose links ONLY from the
URL list I provide. Never invent URLs, and never suggest a link you cannot match
to a line in the list.

Keyword: {{TARGET_KEYWORD}}
Outline:
{{OUTLINE}}

Existing URLs (one per line, "URL — topic"):
{{EXISTING_URLS}}

Propose at most {{MAX_INTERNAL_LINKS}} internal links. For each, pick the outline
section it belongs in and write anchor text that reads naturally — not exact-match
keyword stuffing.

Return a Markdown table:
| Target URL | Relevance to section | Suggested anchor text | Place in section |
- Order by relevance, strongest first.
- If fewer than {{MAX_INTERNAL_LINKS}} URLs are genuinely relevant, return fewer
  and say so. Do not pad the list.

Below the table, list any URL you were unsure about under "Editor: confirm these".
```

**Expected output:** a link table (target URL, relevance, anchor, placement) capped
at {{MAX_INTERNAL_LINKS}} rows, plus an "Editor: confirm these" list.

**Guardrail:** the editor verifies each proposed URL actually exists and is live,
confirms the anchor is not over-optimized, and drops any link that does not serve
the reader. The cap is a ceiling, not a quota.

---

## Prompt 5 — Title & meta variants

**Inputs:**
- {{TARGET_KEYWORD}}
- {{SEARCH_INTENT}} (from Prompt 1)
- {{ANGLE}} (final angle after editor review)
- {{SERP_TITLES}} (the same top-10 titles, so variants can differ from them)

**Prompt:**

```
Write title tag and meta description variants for one article. Optimize for a
click without over-promising — the page must deliver what the title says.

Keyword: {{TARGET_KEYWORD}}
Intent: {{SEARCH_INTENT}}
Angle: {{ANGLE}}
Competing SERP titles (differentiate from these):
{{SERP_TITLES}}

Rules:
- Titles: keep each under {{TITLE_CHAR_CAP}} characters. Include the keyword or a
  close variant near the front. No clickbait, no unverifiable claims.
- Meta descriptions: keep each under {{META_CHAR_CAP}} characters, active voice,
  one clear reason to click.
- Give 3 title options and 2 meta options. Note the character count for each.

Return a Markdown table:
| Type | Variant | Char count | Note (angle / differentiator) |

Flag any variant over the cap instead of silently trimming it.
```

**Expected output:** a table of 3 titles + 2 metas, each with a character count and
a one-line note, with over-cap variants flagged.

**Guardrail:** the editor picks the final title/meta, confirms character counts
against your house limits, and checks that the promise matches the drafted content.
Nothing is locked until the article itself is reviewed.

---

## Assemble the brief (paste outputs into this template)

  # SEO Brief — {{TARGET_KEYWORD}}
  Owner: {{REVIEWER_NAME}}   Date: {{DATE}} (date the brief was assembled)
  Status: DRAFT (pending review)

  ## Intent & angle        (Prompt 1 table)
  ## Outline               (Prompt 2 hierarchy)
  ## Coverage checklist    (Prompt 3 table + must-add items resolved)
  ## Internal links        (Prompt 4 table)
  ## Title & meta          (Prompt 5 table — editor's pick marked)
  ## Word-count target     {{WORD_COUNT_TARGET}}  (estimate)
  ## Handoff prompt        (one paragraph telling the writer the angle,
                            must-include points, and tone)

  Review: [ ] approved by {{REVIEWER_NAME}} on ________
  Status flips to BRIEFED only after this box is checked.

---

## Audit log line (record one per brief)

Append to your brief tracker so any brief is traceable and reproducible:

  TRIGGER: keyword "{{TARGET_KEYWORD}}" entered the brief queue on {{DATE}}
  ACTION:  ran SEO Brief Prompt Pack {{PROMPT_VERSION}} (the pack revision you
           ran, e.g. v1) — prompts 1-5; inputs = SERP titles + URL inventory
           (read-only, pasted)
  IMPACT:  draft brief created; routed to {{REVIEWER_NAME}} for review;
           status = DRAFT until approved

Keep the pasted inputs (SERP titles, URL list) with the log entry so a brief can
be regenerated against the exact data it was built from.
customize: Set the caps ({{MAX_INTERNAL_LINKS}}, {{MAX_H2}}, {{TITLE_CHAR_CAP}}, {{META_CHAR_CAP}}) to your own house standards before first use — they are placeholders, not recommendations
Swap the review gate: name a real {{REVIEWER_NAME}} and decide where DRAFT->BRIEFED status lives (a sheet column, a Slack approval, a tracker field)
Adjust the intent labels in Prompt 1 if your team uses a different rubric (e.g. add 'Local' or 'Comparison')
Point Prompt 4 at a fresh URL export each cycle so the internal-link plan reflects pages that actually exist
Version the pack ({{PROMPT_VERSION}}) whenever you change prompt wording, so the audit line shows which revision produced which brief

items.name: Landing Page Builder Prompt Pack
items.tagline: Five prompts that take a landing page from message-match audit to pre-launch sign-off.
items.what: A five-prompt sequence for building or fixing landing pages that actually match the ads pointing at them: a message-match audit, full wireframe copy for a stated offer and audience, headline and subhead variants tied to your keyword, an objection-driven FAQ built from real sales objections, and a pre-launch CRO checklist review. Every prompt specifies exactly what to paste in and the table format that comes back, so output lands ready to act on. Guardrails are built in: drafts only, no invented proof, and named human approval before anything goes live.
whenToUse: Launching a new paid campaign and the landing page does not exist yet — run prompts 2 through 5 in order
An existing page underperforms and you suspect the ad and the page tell different stories — start with prompt 1
Sales keeps hearing the same objections and the page never answers them — run prompt 4 with real call notes
A page is about to go live and nobody has done a structured final pass — run prompt 5 every time
items.artifact:
# Landing Page Builder Prompt Pack

Five prompts, run in order for a new page or individually for fixes. Each prompt tells you
exactly what to paste in (Inputs), gives you the full prompt text to copy, and pins the
output to a table format you can act on. Fill every placeholder before running.

Works in Claude, Cursor, or any capable AI chat. No account access required — this pack is
copy-in, copy-out. Nothing in it publishes anything. If you run these prompts through an
agent rather than a chat session, the agent operates under your Campaign AGENTS.md scope
rules: pasted copy is a read-only source and every draft is RECOMMEND-tier output.

---

## Session guardrails — paste this once, before Prompt 1

Paste this block at the top of your AI session before running any prompt in the pack.
It sets the operating rules every prompt below relies on. Fill {{APPROVER_ROLE}} (the
human who signs off on sensitive copy and launch — e.g. marketing lead, founder, legal).
If you keep a Campaign CLAUDE.md, paste its voice rules and never-say list after this
block so drafts start on-brand.

```
OPERATING RULES FOR THIS SESSION

1. DRAFT MODE ONLY. Everything you produce is a draft. Never describe copy as
   published, applied, or live. Never suggest auto-publishing anything.
2. NO INVENTED PROOF. Do not fabricate statistics, testimonials, customer
   names, review counts, awards, or performance claims. If a line needs
   evidence I have not pasted into this session, write the line and tag it
   [NEEDS SOURCE].
3. NO PRICING IMPROVISATION. Only state prices, discounts, or guarantees I
   paste verbatim. If pricing copy is needed and missing, tag [NEEDS PRICING].
4. APPROVAL FLAGS. Tag any draft that touches money, guarantees, or legal and
   compliance-sensitive claims (health, finance, "best"/"#1" superlatives,
   competitor comparisons) as [APPROVAL REQUIRED: {{APPROVER_ROLE}}].
5. CHANGE LOGGING. When I tell you a draft was accepted, record it as one
   Trigger / Action / Impact row:
   Trigger (which prompt or finding) | Action (what changed, where, by whom) |
   Impact (page section affected, draft or live).

Confirm you will follow these rules, then wait for my first prompt.
```

---

## Prompt 1 — Message-Match Audit

Diagnoses the gap between what your ad promises and what your page delivers.
Read-only: it produces findings and draft fixes, it changes nothing.

**Inputs:**
- {{AD_COPY}} (every live headline, description, display path, and ad asset, pasted verbatim)
- {{PAGE_COPY}} (the current landing page text, top to bottom in page order, hero first — include button and form labels)
- {{PRIMARY_KEYWORD}} (the search keyword or audience hook the ad targets)
- {{OFFER}} (the specific thing the page asks visitors to take — e.g. free audit, demo, trial)
- {{CONVERSION_ACTION}} (the single action that counts as a conversion — e.g. form submit, booked call)

**Prompt:**

```
You are auditing message match between a paid ad and its landing page. Work
read-only: diagnose and propose, do not rewrite the page wholesale.

CONTEXT
- Primary keyword: {{PRIMARY_KEYWORD}}
- Offer: {{OFFER}}
- Conversion action: {{CONVERSION_ACTION}}

AD COPY (verbatim):
{{AD_COPY}}

LANDING PAGE COPY (verbatim, in page order):
{{PAGE_COPY}}

TASK
1. Extract every promise the ad makes: offer, audience callout, keyword
   phrasing, urgency or price framing (if any), and call to action.
2. For each ad promise, quote the exact page line that delivers it, or mark
   it MISSING. Quote — do not paraphrase.
3. Reverse check: flag anything prominent on the page (hero headline, primary
   CTA, offer framing) that the ad never set up.
4. Rate each gap: BLOCKER (visitor will feel misled or lost), MAJOR (weakens
   the promise), MINOR (polish).

OUTPUT
Table 1 — Ad-to-page match:
| Ad promise (quoted) | Page delivery (quoted line or MISSING) | Match: Exact / Partial / Missing | Severity | Draft fix |

Table 2 — Page elements the ad never set up:
| Page element (quoted) | Why it breaks scent | Draft fix |

Then a one-line verdict — STRONG MATCH / PARTIAL MATCH / MISMATCH — and the
three fixes to make first, ranked by severity.

RULES
- Label every proposed fix DRAFT.
- For any fix touching price, guarantees, or legal claims: propose the
  direction only and tag [APPROVAL REQUIRED: {{APPROVER_ROLE}}] instead of
  writing final copy.
```

**Expected output:** two tables plus a ranked top-3 fix list, as specified in the prompt.

**Guardrail:** this prompt never edits the live page. Fixes ship as drafts; anything
flagged APPROVAL REQUIRED needs sign-off from {{APPROVER_ROLE}} before a human applies it.
Log each applied fix using the change-log rule at the end of this pack.

---

## Prompt 2 — Page Outline and Wireframe Copy

Produces a full section-by-section page draft for a stated offer and audience — real copy
in every section, plus an explicit list of what only you can supply.

**Inputs:**
- {{OFFER}}, {{PRIMARY_KEYWORD}}, {{CONVERSION_ACTION}} (as defined in Prompt 1)
- {{AUDIENCE}} (who lands here: role, situation, what they were searching or scrolling when they clicked)
- {{TRAFFIC_SOURCE}} (where the click comes from — e.g. Google Search, Meta, LinkedIn — it changes how much context the visitor arrives with)
- {{PROOF_ASSETS}} (testimonials, client names or logos you may use, case study summaries — paste the actual text; write NONE if you have none)
- {{TOP_OBJECTIONS}} (the 3-5 most common reasons prospects hesitate, in their words)
- {{BRAND_VOICE}} (two or three adjectives plus one sentence you consider on-voice)

**Prompt:**

```
Draft wireframe copy for a landing page. Every section gets real draft copy,
not lorem ipsum or notes-to-self.

CONTEXT
- Offer: {{OFFER}}
- Audience: {{AUDIENCE}}
- Primary keyword: {{PRIMARY_KEYWORD}}
- Traffic source: {{TRAFFIC_SOURCE}}
- Conversion action: {{CONVERSION_ACTION}}
- Brand voice: {{BRAND_VOICE}}
- Proof assets available (use ONLY these): {{PROOF_ASSETS}}
- Known objections: {{TOP_OBJECTIONS}}

TASK
Draft the page as a numbered section table, top to bottom:
1. Hero: headline, subhead, primary CTA button label.
2. Context: name the visitor's problem or situation in their own words.
3. How it works: 3-4 steps or components of {{OFFER}}.
4. Proof: built ONLY from {{PROOF_ASSETS}}. If NONE, design the section
   structure anyway and tag every proof line [NEEDS SOURCE].
5. Objection handling: address the top items from {{TOP_OBJECTIONS}}.
6. Final CTA: restate offer and CTA, and say what happens after conversion.
7. Form/CTA microcopy: field labels, button text, one reassurance line.

OUTPUT
| # | Section | Job it does | Draft copy | What I must supply |

After the table, list every [NEEDS SOURCE] and [NEEDS PRICING] tag in one
checklist so nothing unverified slips into the build.

RULES
- The hero must reflect {{PRIMARY_KEYWORD}} and the framing of the ad or post
  driving traffic from {{TRAFFIC_SOURCE}}.
- One conversion action; do not add secondary CTAs.
- No invented statistics, names, or endorsements anywhere.
```

**Expected output:** one wireframe table (7 rows) plus a consolidated NEEDS SOURCE / NEEDS
PRICING checklist.

**Guardrail:** the draft is a build spec, not a live page. Nothing goes into your CMS
or page builder until the NEEDS SOURCE checklist is cleared and {{APPROVER_ROLE}} has
signed off on any pricing, guarantee, or comparison copy.

---

## Prompt 3 — Headline and Subhead Variants

Generates ten headline + subhead pairs tied to the keyword the visitor just searched or
the hook they just clicked — with a risk flag on every variant.

**Inputs:**
- {{PRIMARY_KEYWORD}}, {{OFFER}}, {{AUDIENCE}}, {{BRAND_VOICE}} (as defined above)
- {{AD_HEADLINE}} (the exact headline of the ad this page receives traffic from)
- {{BANNED_PHRASES}} (words or claims you never use — competitor names, superlatives legal has rejected, category cliches; write NONE if none)
- {{REAL_DEADLINE}} (an actual expiry date or capacity limit if one exists; write NONE and urgency variants will be skipped)

**Prompt:**

```
Write 10 headline + subhead pairs for a landing page.

CONTEXT
- Primary keyword: {{PRIMARY_KEYWORD}}
- Ad headline the visitor just clicked: {{AD_HEADLINE}}
- Offer: {{OFFER}}
- Audience: {{AUDIENCE}}
- Brand voice: {{BRAND_VOICE}}
- Banned phrases: {{BANNED_PHRASES}}
- Real deadline or limit (NONE means skip urgency angles): {{REAL_DEADLINE}}

TASK
Cover these angles, at least one pair each:
- Keyword echo: mirrors {{PRIMARY_KEYWORD}} and {{AD_HEADLINE}} closely.
- Outcome: the end state the visitor wants.
- Objection flip: leads with the hesitation, then answers it.
- How-it-works: the concrete mechanism of {{OFFER}}.
- Question: the question the visitor is already asking.
- Urgency: ONLY if {{REAL_DEADLINE}} is not NONE, and only using that fact.

Headlines under 10 words. Subheads under 20 words and adding new information,
never restating the headline.

OUTPUT
| # | Angle | Headline | Subhead | Keyword present? (Y/N) | Risk flag |

Risk flag values: CLEAN (safe to test), NEEDS SOURCE (implies proof I have
not supplied), APPROVAL REQUIRED (touches pricing, guarantees, comparisons,
or regulated claims).

Close with your top 3 picks for a first test and one sentence each on why.

RULES
- No superlatives ("best", "#1", "fastest") unless I pasted evidence.
- No fabricated urgency or scarcity.
- Nothing from {{BANNED_PHRASES}}.
```

**Expected output:** one 10-row table plus a top-3 shortlist with rationale.

**Guardrail:** variants are test candidates, not replacements. Anything flagged NEEDS
SOURCE or APPROVAL REQUIRED is blocked from testing until cleared by {{APPROVER_ROLE}}.
If you A/B test headlines with an automation tool, create the test in draft or paused
state by default and require a human to start it.

---

## Prompt 4 — Objection and FAQ Section

Turns real objections from sales calls, chat logs, and support tickets into an FAQ section
that answers what visitors are actually worried about — using only policies you actually have.

**Inputs:**
- {{SALES_OBJECTIONS}} (verbatim objections from calls, CRM notes, chat, or tickets — paste 5-10, exactly as prospects said them)
- {{POLICY_TEXT}} (your actual refund, guarantee, contract, cancellation, and data-handling terms, pasted verbatim; write NONE for any you lack)
- {{OFFER}}, {{CONVERSION_ACTION}} (as defined above)

**Prompt:**

```
Build an objection-handling FAQ section for a landing page from real sales
objections.

CONTEXT
- Offer: {{OFFER}}
- Conversion action: {{CONVERSION_ACTION}}
- Verbatim objections from real prospects:
{{SALES_OBJECTIONS}}
- Official policy text (the ONLY source you may cite for commitments):
{{POLICY_TEXT}}

TASK
1. Group duplicate objections; keep the most vivid verbatim phrasing.
2. Rewrite each as an FAQ question in the customer's own words — "What if it
   doesn't work for us?", not "What is our satisfaction commitment?".
3. Draft an answer of 80 words or less: direct first sentence, then the
   reason to believe. Cite {{POLICY_TEXT}} where relevant.
4. Mark each answer's claim status.

OUTPUT
| Objection (verbatim) | FAQ question | Answer draft | Claim status | Placement |

Claim status values: SUPPORTED (backed by pasted policy), NEEDS SOURCE
(needs proof I have not supplied), APPROVAL REQUIRED (commits money, a
guarantee, or a legal/data promise).
Placement values: FAQ section / near CTA / near pricing.

Close with the recommended question order (most conversion-critical first)
and any objection the main page copy should absorb rather than the FAQ.

RULES
- Never invent a guarantee, refund term, timeline, or security commitment
  that is not in {{POLICY_TEXT}}.
- If {{POLICY_TEXT}} is NONE for an objection, state what policy would need
  to exist and tag the answer [NEEDS SOURCE] — do not improvise one.
```

**Expected output:** one table (one row per unique objection) plus an ordering
recommendation.

**Guardrail:** every answer that commits your company to anything — refunds,
guarantees, timelines, data handling — carries APPROVAL REQUIRED and must be verified
against real policy by {{APPROVER_ROLE}} before publishing. An FAQ that promises what your
contract does not is a liability, not a conversion asset.

---

## Prompt 5 — Pre-Launch CRO Checklist Review

A structured final pass before the page goes live. The model checks; a named human
launches. Run it on the staging or draft version, never as post-launch cleanup.

**Inputs:**
- {{FINAL_PAGE_COPY}} (the complete near-final page text in order, including form fields, button labels, and footer trust elements)
- {{AD_COPY}} and {{PRIMARY_KEYWORD}} (as defined in Prompt 1)
- {{FORM_FIELDS}} (every field on the form and whether each is required)
- {{TRACKING_PLAN}} (the events you intend to fire — e.g. form submit, thank-you pageview — and the tool that fires them)
- {{PAGE_URL}} (the URL the page will go live at)

**Prompt:**

```
Run a pre-launch review of this landing page. You are the reviewer, not the
approver: your output is a findings report, and a named human decides launch.

INPUTS
- Final page copy: {{FINAL_PAGE_COPY}}
- Ad copy it must match: {{AD_COPY}}
- Primary keyword: {{PRIMARY_KEYWORD}}
- Form fields and required status: {{FORM_FIELDS}}
- Tracking plan: {{TRACKING_PLAN}}

CHECKS — evaluate each and quote your evidence from the inputs:
- Message match: hero reflects {{PRIMARY_KEYWORD}} and the ad promise; offer
  and CTA match the ad.
- Clarity: a first-time visitor can say what this is, who it is for, and
  what to do next from the hero alone.
- CTA: one conversion action; the button label says what happens next; the
  CTA repeats after proof and FAQ.
- Friction: every required form field is justified by the offer's value; no
  field asks for data the page never explains needing.
- Proof integrity: every statistic, name, and testimonial has a source; no
  [NEEDS SOURCE] or [NEEDS PRICING] tags remain anywhere.
- Trust and compliance: pricing and guarantee lines match approved policy;
  a privacy link sits near the form; no unapproved superlatives or
  competitor comparisons.
- Tracking: {{TRACKING_PLAN}} covers the conversion action; test and staging
  traffic is excluded or noted as a known gap.

OUTPUT
| Check | Result: Pass / Flag / Fail | Evidence (quoted) | Fix | Launch blocker? (Y/N) |

Then the verdict: READY / READY WITH FIXES (list them) / NOT READY (list the
blockers). End with this sign-off block for the human approver to complete:

  LAUNCH SIGN-OFF
  Verdict accepted by {{APPROVER_ROLE}} — name: ______  date: ______
  Blockers resolved or explicitly waived: ______
  Log entry — Trigger: pre-launch review | Action: page published to
  {{PAGE_URL}} | Impact: live traffic begins on this page
```

**Expected output:** one checklist table, a verdict line, and a fill-in sign-off block.

**Guardrail:** the verdict is advisory. The page does not go live on a READY verdict —
it goes live when {{APPROVER_ROLE}} completes the sign-off block. Keep the completed block
with your log: it is the audit record of who launched what, when, and which flags were waived.

---

## Change-log rule (applies to the whole pack)

Whenever a draft from any prompt is applied to a real page, record one
Trigger / Action / Impact row:

| Trigger | Action | Impact |
|---|---|---|
| Prompt 1 audit: hero missing {{OFFER}} | Hero headline replaced (by: name, date) | {{PAGE_URL}} hero section — live |
| Prompt 4: refund objection unanswered | FAQ item added after policy verified | {{PAGE_URL}} FAQ section — live |

Keep the log wherever your team already keeps campaign records. The habit matters more
than the tool: every change traceable to a trigger, every action attributable to a person,
every impact stated before it happens.

---

## Placeholder reference

| Placeholder | What it is |
|---|---|
| {{APPROVER_ROLE}} | Human who signs off on sensitive copy and launch |
| {{AD_COPY}} | Live ad headlines, descriptions, and assets, verbatim |
| {{PAGE_COPY}} | Current page text, top to bottom |
| {{PRIMARY_KEYWORD}} | Keyword or hook the ad targets |
| {{OFFER}} | The specific thing visitors get |
| {{CONVERSION_ACTION}} | The one action that counts as a conversion |
| {{AUDIENCE}} | Who lands on the page and why |
| {{TRAFFIC_SOURCE}} | Channel the click comes from |
| {{PROOF_ASSETS}} | Real testimonials, logos, case studies (or NONE) |
| {{TOP_OBJECTIONS}} | Common hesitations, in prospects' words |
| {{BRAND_VOICE}} | Voice adjectives plus one on-voice sentence |
| {{AD_HEADLINE}} | Exact headline of the connected ad |
| {{BANNED_PHRASES}} | Words or claims you never use (or NONE) |
| {{REAL_DEADLINE}} | Actual expiry or capacity limit (or NONE) |
| {{SALES_OBJECTIONS}} | Verbatim objections from calls and tickets |
| {{POLICY_TEXT}} | Actual refund/guarantee/contract terms (or NONE) |
| {{FINAL_PAGE_COPY}} | Complete near-final page text |
| {{FORM_FIELDS}} | Every form field plus required status |
| {{TRACKING_PLAN}} | Events you will fire and the tool that fires them |
| {{PAGE_URL}} | Where the page goes live |
customize: Swap the approval categories in guardrail rule 4 for your industry — regulated sectors should add their own compliance list
Add or remove wireframe sections in Prompt 2 to match your page builder's block library
Tune Prompt 3's angle list to your channel: keyword echo matters most for search, hook echo for paid social
Feed Prompt 4 from whatever objection source you trust — call recordings, CRM loss reasons, or chat transcripts
Extend Prompt 5's checklist with your own launch requirements, such as accessibility, page speed, or legal review
items.metaDescription: Landing page prompt pack: message-match audit, wireframe copy, headline variants, objection FAQs, and a pre-launch CRO checklist with guardrails built in.

items.name: Campaign Launch Kit Template
items.tagline: One fill-in document from brief to approved launch — nothing spends until sign-off.
items.what: The Campaign Launch Kit is the single document a team fills in for every paid campaign: brief, ad group and keyword structure, copy variants with character limits, UTM taxonomy, tracking verification, QA, sign-off, and a first-week review schedule. It exists because launch failures are usually process failures — a missing conversion tag, a hand-typed UTM, a campaign enabled that nobody approved. The kit turns each of those into a checklist line with an owner, and keeps every campaign paused until a named human signs off.
whenToUse: You are launching a new paid campaign and want structure, tracking, and approvals in one place
Your launches each look slightly different and downstream reporting keeps breaking
You are handing launches to a new hire, an agency, or an AI agent and need the gates written down
A past campaign spent before tracking was verified and you never want that again
items.artifact:
# CAMPAIGN LAUNCH KIT

Campaign: {{CAMPAIGN_NAME}} (internal campaign name — build it from the naming convention in Section 5, never freehand)
Owner: {{CAMPAIGN_OWNER}} (the one person accountable for this launch end to end)
Channel: {{CHANNEL}} (e.g. Google Search — duplicate this kit per channel rather than mixing channels in one)
Kit last edited: {{KIT_DATE}} (update every time this document changes)
Status: [ ] Draft   [ ] Built   [ ] QA passed   [ ] Approved   [ ] Live

**How to use this kit**
1. Fill Section 1 completely before building anything. A blank brief field is a blocker, not a note for later.
2. Set the guardrails in Section 2 before the campaign exists anywhere in the ad account.
3. Build Sections 3–5, verify Section 6, run Section 7.
4. Everything uploads PAUSED. Only the named approver in Section 8 enables spend.
5. Log every account-touching change in Section 10 as it happens, not after.

This document is the source of truth. If the account and the kit disagree, the launch stops until they match.

---

## 1. Campaign Brief

Every field is required. If a field cannot be filled, the campaign is not ready to build.

| Field | Entry |
|---|---|
| Goal (one sentence) | {{CAMPAIGN_GOAL}} (what this campaign exists to do, e.g. "generate demo requests for the Q3 offer") |
| Primary KPI | {{PRIMARY_KPI}} (the single number that defines success, and the system it is measured in) |
| Secondary KPI | {{SECONDARY_KPI}} (supporting signal — leading indicator or quality check) |
| Audience / ICP | {{AUDIENCE}} (who, in one or two sentences — role, segment, geography) |
| Offer | {{OFFER}} (the exact thing every ad promises: demo, guide, trial, discount) |
| Landing page(s) | {{LANDING_URL}} (final URL(s) — must be live before QA, not "will be live by launch") |
| Competitive angle | {{ANGLE}} (why someone picks this offer over the obvious alternative) |
| Daily budget cap | {{DAILY_CAP}} (a number you set and commit to — this is a placeholder, not a suggestion) |
| Total flight cap | {{TOTAL_CAP}} (the maximum this campaign may spend across the full flight) |
| Flight dates | {{START_DATE}} to {{END_DATE}} |
| Geo / language | {{GEO_LANGUAGE}} (targeted locations and languages, plus explicit location exclusions) |
| Exclusions | {{EXCLUSIONS}} (audiences, placements, topics, or terms this campaign must avoid) |
| Approver | {{APPROVER_NAME}} (the named human who enables spend — must not be the person who built the campaign) |

---

## 2. Guardrails — Set Before Anything Is Built

Confirm each guardrail before Section 3 starts. QA (Section 7) re-checks all of them.
If your team keeps a signed Guardrail Configuration for this account, the caps and
scopes below must match it — that document wins any disagreement.

| # | Guardrail | Rule | Confirmed |
|---|---|---|---|
| G1 | Paused by default | Every campaign, ad group, and ad uploads in a paused state. No exceptions, including "just to test." | [ ] |
| G2 | Caps set at creation | {{DAILY_CAP}} is entered on the campaign before upload, not added after enable. | [ ] |
| G3 | Cap raises are human-only | Any budget increase requires a new sign-off row in Section 8 before it is applied. No tool, script, or agent may raise a cap. | [ ] |
| G4 | Separated duties | Builder, QA reviewer, and approver: at least the builder and approver are different people. Only {{APPROVER_NAME}} enables spend. | [ ] |
| G5 | Tool and agent scope | Any automation connected to the account may create paused entities only. All other account access is read-only until a human widens it in writing — in the Guardrail Configuration or the agent's Campaign AGENTS.md, not in a prompt. | [ ] |
| G6 | Change logging | Every account change — human or automated — gets a Trigger / Action / Impact row in Section 10. | [ ] |
| G7 | Kill switch | {{KILL_SWITCH}} (who can pause everything and the exact steps, e.g. "owner pauses the campaign at campaign level; approver notified same day") | [ ] |

---

## 3. Ad Group / Keyword Structure

Ad group naming pattern: {{ADGROUP_PATTERN}} (e.g. theme–intent–matchtype; write the pattern here and follow it in every row)

| Ad group name | Theme | Intent tier | Match type | Seed keywords | Ad-group negatives | Landing page |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| {{ADGROUP_1}} | {{THEME_1}} (one theme only) | High / Mid / Low | Exact / Phrase / Broad | {{KEYWORDS_1}} (the seed list, one intent) | {{NEGATIVES_1}} (negatives specific to this group) | {{LP_1}} (this group's final URL) |
| {{ADGROUP_2}} | | | | | | |
| {{ADGROUP_3}} | | | | | | |
| {{ADGROUP_4}} | | | | | | |

Campaign-level negative list: {{NEGATIVE_LIST}} (shared negatives applied to the whole campaign — brand exclusions, irrelevant intents, existing-customer terms)

Structure rules:
- One theme per ad group. If a keyword fits two groups, it lives in the higher-intent group and is negated in the other.
- Every ad group maps to exactly one landing page whose content matches that group's intent.
- Broad match is allowed only with a monitoring commitment: the day-1 search-term check in Section 9 is scheduled before launch, not after.

---

## 4. Copy Variant Matrix

Character limits below are for Google Ads responsive search ads: headlines max 30 characters, descriptions max 90 characters, display path fields max 15 characters each. If you are launching on another channel, replace these limits with that channel's limits before writing a word. Voice, claims, and never-say rules live in your Campaign CLAUDE.md if you keep one — copy that fails it fails QA.

Complete one matrix per ad group. Copy this block for each.

**Ad group: {{ADGROUP_1}}**

Headline bank (max 30 characters each — count before upload, QA recounts):

| Slot | Headline | Char count | Pinned? |
|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | {{HEADLINE_1}} (lead with the offer or the intent behind the group's keywords) | /30 | |
| H2 | {{HEADLINE_2}} | /30 | |
| H3 | {{HEADLINE_3}} | /30 | |
| H4 | {{HEADLINE_4}} | /30 | |
| H5 | {{HEADLINE_5}} | /30 | |
| H6 | {{HEADLINE_6}} | /30 | |
| H7 | {{HEADLINE_7}} | /30 | |
| H8 | {{HEADLINE_8}} | /30 | |

Description bank (max 90 characters each):

| Slot | Description | Char count |
|---|---|---|
| D1 | {{DESCRIPTION_1}} (state the offer and the next step plainly) | /90 |
| D2 | {{DESCRIPTION_2}} | /90 |
| D3 | {{DESCRIPTION_3}} | /90 |
| D4 | {{DESCRIPTION_4}} | /90 |

Display paths: /{{PATH_1}} (max 15 chars) /{{PATH_2}} (max 15 chars)

Variant assembly — three ads per ad group, each with a distinct angle:

| Variant | Headlines used | Descriptions used | Angle |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | | | {{ANGLE_A}} (e.g. offer-led: what they get) |
| B | | | {{ANGLE_B}} (e.g. benefit-led: what changes for them) |
| C | | | {{ANGLE_C}} (e.g. proof-led: why believe it) |

Copy rules:
- Every claim must be substantiated. Source for claims used in this campaign: {{CLAIMS_SOURCE}} (doc or folder where proof lives). If a claim cannot be sourced, it does not ship.
- The ad's promise must appear above the fold on its landing page. QA checks this parity ad by ad.
- No superlatives you cannot prove; no competitor trademarks unless cleared against platform policy and noted here.

---

## 5. UTM Plan

This table IS the taxonomy. URLs are built from it by formula — nobody hand-types a UTM. A new value for any parameter is added to this table first, then used.

| Parameter | Convention | Allowed values | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| utm_source | Platform serving the ad, lowercase | {{SOURCE_VALUES}} (closed list, e.g. google, bing, meta, linkedin) | google |
| utm_medium | Buying model, lowercase | {{MEDIUM_VALUES}} (closed list, e.g. cpc, paid-social, display) | cpc |
| utm_campaign | {{CAMPAIGN_UTM_PATTERN}} (your pattern, e.g. geo-product-intent-yyyymm) | Built from the pattern only | ca-launchkit-brand-202607 |
| utm_content | Ad group + variant letter | Matches Section 3 names | brand-exact-a |
| utm_term | Keyword, or the platform's keyword insertion parameter | Matches Section 3 keywords | campaign-launch-kit |

Formatting rules: lowercase everywhere; one separator (hyphen recommended) and never a mix; no spaces; no PII; no internal codenames you would not want visible in a URL bar.

Final URL register — one row per ad, filled before QA:

| Ad group | Variant | Final URL with UTMs | Resolves 200 (no redirect stripping params)? |
|---|---|---|---|
| {{ADGROUP_1}} | A | | [ ] |
| {{ADGROUP_1}} | B | | [ ] |
| {{ADGROUP_1}} | C | | [ ] |

---

## 6. Tracking Checklist — Verified Before Any Spend

Rule: no spend is enabled until every row below reads Verified. "It should work" is not verified. A test conversion is.

| Conversion action | Where it fires | Primary / secondary | Test method | Verified by | Date |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| {{CONVERSION_ACTION}} (e.g. demo form submit) | {{FIRE_LOCATION}} (page or event that triggers it) | Primary | Live test submission + tag debugger | | |
| {{CONVERSION_ACTION_2}} (a secondary action, if any) | | Secondary | | | |

- [ ] Each conversion action fired at least once in a live test and appeared in the ads platform
- [ ] Conversion counting (one per click vs every) matches the Primary KPI definition in Section 1
- [ ] One test click completed end to end: UTM parameters survived all redirects and the session landed in analytics with source/medium intact
- [ ] The analytics property receiving the data is the one the Primary KPI is measured in
- [ ] Ads platform and analytics both recorded the test conversion (counts may differ; both must record it)
- [ ] Consent behavior tested: with consent declined, tags behave according to your consent policy

---

## 7. Pre-Launch QA Checklist

Blockers stop the launch until resolved. Warnings are acknowledged in writing by the reviewer — never cleared silently.

**Blockers — all must pass:**
- [ ] Every Section 1 brief field is complete
- [ ] Every final URL in Section 5 resolves correctly — no 404s, no redirect that strips UTMs
- [ ] Every headline and description recounted against the character limits in Section 4 — recounted, not trusted
- [ ] Every claim in every ad traced to {{CLAIMS_SOURCE}}
- [ ] Landing page matches each ad's promise above the fold
- [ ] Targeting matches the brief exactly: geo, language, audiences, exclusions
- [ ] Campaign daily budget in the account equals {{DAILY_CAP}} from Section 1 exactly
- [ ] Every campaign, ad group, and ad is paused in the account
- [ ] Section 6 tracking checklist fully verified
- [ ] Guardrails G1–G7 all confirmed
- [ ] Naming in the account matches Section 3 and Section 5 patterns character for character

**Warnings — note and acknowledge with initials:**
- [ ] Broad match keywords present — day-1 search-term check scheduled (Section 9) — ack: ____
- [ ] Keyword overlap between ad groups noted and negated per Section 3 rules — ack: ____
- [ ] Landing page load time checked and noted — ack: ____
- [ ] {{CHANNEL_SPECIFIC_WARNING}} (add channel-specific items: extensions, placements, audience expansion settings) — ack: ____

QA result:  Blockers open: ____ (must be 0)   Warnings acknowledged: ____ by ____   Date: ____

---

## 8. Launch Sign-Off

| Role | Name | Date | Time | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Built by | | | | |
| QA reviewed by | | | | Must confirm: 0 blockers, all warnings acknowledged |
| Approved by | {{APPROVER_NAME}} | | | See approval statement below |
| Enabled in account by | | | | The moment spend actually turned on |

Approval statement (the approver signs this, not a vague "looks good"):

> I approve {{CAMPAIGN_NAME}} to spend up to {{DAILY_CAP}} per day and {{TOTAL_CAP}} in total, from {{START_DATE}} to {{END_DATE}}, with the targeting and copy documented in this kit. Any change that increases spend, widens targeting, or edits live copy requires re-approval before it is applied.
>
> Signed: ______________  Date: ______  Time: ______

Rollback: before enable, the paused build can be deleted with nothing spent. After enable, pausing is one action — the kill-switch owner and steps are in G7.

---

## 9. Day-1 / Week-1 Review Schedule

Set the act-if thresholds now, before launch — they are decision triggers you commit to in advance, not benchmarks. Every value below is yours to choose.

| Checkpoint | When | What to check | Act-if threshold | Action if breached | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Serving check | Launch + {{HOURS_AFTER_ENABLE}} (how long after enable you first check, e.g. "2–4 hours") | Ads approved and serving; spend pacing vs {{DAILY_CAP}}; impressions registering | {{PACING_THRESHOLD}} (your trigger, e.g. pacing to exceed the daily cap) | Pause, investigate, log in Section 10 | {{CAMPAIGN_OWNER}} |
| Day-1 close | End of first day | Conversion tag received real data (or clicks with zero conversions explained); search terms scanned for junk; UTM sessions landing in analytics | Clicks recorded but tracking silent | Automatic pause until tracking confirmed — data first, spend second | |
| Day-3 | 72 hours in | Search-term review, add negatives; ad disapprovals; spend pacing vs {{TOTAL_CAP}} | {{DAY3_THRESHOLD}} (your trigger, e.g. share of irrelevant search terms) | Add negatives / tighten match types; log changes | |
| Week-1 | Day 7 | Pacing vs total cap; directional read on the Primary KPI; warnings from Section 7 revisited | — | Decision: continue / adjust / pause. Decision and reasoning logged in Section 10 | {{APPROVER_NAME}} |

Standing rule: any checkpoint that finds spend running without verified tracking is an automatic pause, no approval needed to stop — only to restart.

---

## 10. Launch Log — Trigger / Action / Impact

Every account-touching change gets a row, whether a human or a tool made it. This is the audit trail your approver, your client, or your future self reads.

| Timestamp | Trigger (what prompted it) | Action (what changed, by whom or what tool) | Impact (spend / targeting / tracking affected) | Approved by |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| {{TIMESTAMP}} (date and time of the entry) | Brief signed off (Section 1) | Campaign structure uploaded paused via {{UPLOAD_TOOL}} (e.g. Google Ads Editor) | None — all entities paused | {{INITIALS}} (initials of the person logging) |
| | QA passed, 0 blockers (Section 7) | Approval requested from {{APPROVER_NAME}} | None | |
| | Approval granted (Section 8) | Campaign enabled | Spend begins under {{DAILY_CAP}} daily cap | {{APPROVER_NAME}} |
| | | | | |
| | | | | |

Filing: when the flight ends, complete the log, mark the kit status, and archive it with the campaign name. The next campaign starts by copying this kit — conventions, guardrails, and all.
